back: A system where the legislature consists of two houses (e.g., Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha).
The right of all adult citizens to vote regardless of gender, social status, or religion.
The Lower House of India's Parliament, directly elected by the people for a 5-year term.
The Upper House of India's Parliament, representing the states; its members are indirectly elected.
The Vice President of India, who presides over the Rajya Sabha by virtue of their office.
A draft proposal of a law; it becomes an Act after passing through Parliament and receiving Presidential assent.
A bill restricted to financial matters; it can only originate in the Lok Sabha.
A law passed by the Parliament and signed by the President.
The specific ministry or government department assigned to a minister.
The team of ministers headed by the Prime Minister that governs the country.
The principle that all ministers are together responsible for government decisions and actions.
Permanent parliamentary groups that scrutinize policies and bills in detail.
The President of India.
The Prime Minister of India, who wields real power.
Matters on which only the central government can make laws (e.g., Defense).
Matters on which only state governments can make laws (e.g., Police).
Matters on which both Union and State governments can make laws (e.g., Education).
The Legislative Assembly of a state; it is always the lower house.
The Legislative Council; present only in bicameral states as the upper house.
The branch of government that interprets and applies laws and ensures checks and balances.
The first hour of a parliamentary sitting used by MPs to question ministers.
The public journal where newly enacted Acts are officially published.
The actual hours worked by the Parliament versus the time scheduled.
